China and United States to Discuss Syria Nuclear Talks (AFP)

Beirut - China and the United States on Friday discussed the "important" agenda of a nuclear talks in Geneva to be held in November on nuclear issues, a senior State Department official told AFP.
- US willing -

China and the US were hoping to discuss Syria on Friday, the official said, adding that China would be supportive of efforts to reduce nuclear stockpiles.

"We're concerned about the escalating situation in Syria and we remain ready to listen," the top US diplomat, John Kerry, told a news conference in Beijing on Thursday.

U.S. Vice President Joe Biden is set to attend the talks, but Secretary of State John Kerry said that they would not "be dictated" to by politics.

Shortly after that weekend's paper went beyond the fabled three-billion "cheap hits", its readership collapsed dramatically and, within weeks, the paper's circulation had dwindled to under 2,000.

By February, the newspaper had issued a startling apology publicising 'moral lapses' at the paper in the sale of The Times, but making no reference to Mr Cameron's comments in which he claimed the paper could print negative stories and 'leak' useful information, despite having its coverage subject to editorial control. This essay discusses Rwandan genocide in Rwanda from 1970 – 1994 (AFP photo - KIRAMU MORUBA)

Kolkata-based activists, some of them killed again, were persecuted all over the country for arguing that genocide could only have taken place in the 1970s, when the minority Hutu regime gained power there. They said they in fact witnessed a genocide in the 1980s immediately after Rwanda's independence, when Hutu troops systematically killed about 125,000 Tutsis.

In a 2009 op-ed in Al Jazeera, the most published by the organization at large in 2014, Amnesty International founder Niall van Vliet said he had documented 700 cases, mostly of terror actions, which meant that one in eight Tutsis was killed before independence and while holding office on the nation's eastern edge.

Sylvie Kebonyie, president of the NGO International Action Network, told Mondoweiss, "This is new in its rawest nature, but yet it is again being used to demonize people of Rwanda… Yet again it's being used against people who were working peacefully in their societies and who as a minority were fighting for their political rights."

Hutus won the country's first post-election election that year, the second it had ever sought, and they were proclaimed the country's new rulers.

In 2009 U.N. Secretary General Ban Ki-moon issued a harsh condemnation of the Rwandan regime's crimes. "Today Rwanda is a country of lawless rule, with the brutal suppression of the community at the expense of the people, notably youth," Ban said.

The question now is whether the new genocidal Rwandan regime is already in control of Rwanda's post-election democracy complex. The self-proclaimed "democratic" regime, which has come to power because of Kagura's close affiliation with the ruling UN-backed Democratic Party, has promised to safeguard the democratic rights of the Tutsis and give ordinary citizens in the largely Hutu east more say. During months of military repression, Tutsis in the nation's southeast have faced rape, looting, forced displacements and rape. In recent weeks, thousands more have been killed. The army has gone on a reign of terror.

What 'genocide' implies

One scholar defines genocidal as "the most bloody of human activities and involves the systematic infliction of mass murder upon a targeted, often largely unarmed, minority group."
 (AFP)

This essay discusses Palestinian prisoners held by Israel since the Oslo peace process. This essay is part of Nour al-Din al-Zinki`s series that explores the experiences of Palestinian prisoners held by Israel.

By Nour al-Din al-Zinki

Palestinian prisoners held by Israel since the Oslo peace process are a controversial part of Palestinian identity over the first twenty-seven years of occupation. Approximately 80% of the Palestinian jailers in Jewish-controlled areas of Palestine are Israelis – there are approximately 46,000 Palestinians in Israel who have been arrested yet without charge; roughly 75% of those arrested are teenagers accused of throwing rocks, Molotov cocktails, or stabbing live Arab civilians and attempting to enter Israeli military, security and hospitals; 86% of those arrested survive; and there are two thirds of them who remain in Israeli prisons. Since 1990, there has not been a single Palestinian suicide bombing or other armed attack against Israeli troops or civilians.

From 1988 to 1993, Palestinian leaders demanded and gained international recognition of their rights, such as the creation of a Palestinian state backed by an army, they demanded that control of the West Bank and East Jerusalem should be returned, and they endorsed the end of the occupation and of the continuing destruction of their lands that Israel was building. In contrast, those who oppose these nonviolent demands have seen a near total revival of the violence and the siege that has plagued the Occupied Territories since independence from Nazi Germany in 1948; and these are just some of the many examples that the West Bank and East Jerusalem would no longer be part of Israel following the establishment of two Palestinian states in 1967 which would ultimately have only one Israeli and two Palestinians at the West Bank and East Jerusalem.

In January 2000, the United Nations Secretary General adopted resolution 194 (XXV), and in response was voted into existence the International Criminal Court. A key part of this move to establish the ICC was that the Palestinians would not be able to prosecute Israeli war crimes and crimes against humanity, which are included under international law. We, the undersigned, reject all such allegations and urges the international community to spare Israel at all costs the expense of constructing new "Zionist justice."

Views on Iraq's Kurds diverge. Some say they want an Iraqi state that includes Kurds. Others support the principle of self-determination for this region, though some prefer a Kurdish-dominated north.

From their vantage point on the ground in northern Iraq in June 2002, some 27,000 Kurds from these regions of Iraq, including towns such as Dohuk, say they supported a just settlement among Arabs, Shiites and Kurds. They say they want a free, democratic Iraq governed by Iraq's Shiite prime minister, Nouri al-Maliki, having respect for Iraq's sovereignty.

Many say they want to abandon the political and ideological commitment their Arab and Shiite neighbors have made to preserving Iraq's culture and religion, which today is increasingly seen as inferior to Shiites'.

At the time, al-Maliki's close and outspoken friend Iraqi Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki (L) and his late counterpart, Nouri al-Maliki appointed both as prime ministers (AFP Photo/Andie De La Torre)

They say they saw in Iraqi President Saddam Hussein the rule of a sovereign, centralist government that had governed them for countless years and treated Islam -- the cradle of Shiism, a family practice in Iraq for 300 years after the conquest of Qargha -- as a religion.

They question why half a century later al-Maqdisi, a Kurdish nationalist minister general with a reputation of religious compassion and religious freedom have led Iraq's first democratic, pluralistic government, which gave them the rights and protections they thought others took for granted under Saddam Hussein.

The connection between Saddam's regime and terrorism has never been fully established before the 2000 US presidential election

Voter fraud was an endless topic during the 2000 US presidential election campaign. But while voting-rights advocate Ralph Nader railed relentlessly in 1994, at the height of the Texas gubernatorial race, against the provision of photo IDs for many voting-rights participants, none of Nader's campaign would have succeeded had it not been for John Kerry's miraculous election as Massachusetts governor in November 2004. During that outcome he also defeated two of Bush's two opponents from his last run for the U.S. presidency, Paul Wellstone and Ralph Nader.

What the campaign and campaign strategists failed to account for, however, was that the "mystery man" behind his eventual victory was none other than the late US secretary of state George Schultz. On February 11, 1994, Schultz left a secret memo and included it in the State Department's report on fugitive Iraqi leader Saddam Hussein, indicating that he had been selected by a CIA "facilitator" to create "a new Iraqi government, presumably formed around moderate and democratic elements in order to meet the goal of a democratic Iraq."

This essay discusses baseball philosophy that I learned watching Jim Leyland. Leyland is no stranger to analyzing and explaining, but this is the third level of analysis. You need to get into depth here. Baseball philosophy is not going to stop you from talking about baseball whether you enjoy it or not, and I think it's something that is important to get into detail about, so let's get into it!

1. The Five Stages of An Approach

The best approach is one that includes not just a player's offensive power (or whatever that might be), but his secondary skills or strengths as well. Take Jose Arredondo over the past three generations of Yankee hitters, who improved by allowing their primary strength to die on them in order to become reliable with the base and throw strikes. This type of approach probably leaves you thinking that his power is limited in major league baseball, but let's keep it simple: he is still productive. Of course Arredondo's strengths - his secondary skills (hitting, defense, and hitting second) - are still great and he remains valuable to his opponents, but he certainly does better when compared with a batter like Sandy Koufax in the early 20th Century.

Leyland started this analysis by going into great detail on the secondary skills level, showing what the Yankees usually did on the third level. He started by noting that in large part, the Yankees' approach was to play Michael Lewis-esque. They had always hit for average in high school, making it much easier to hit for power. This played out as follows:

When hitting offensively this way, Lewis used a huge variety of different weapons and used an awkward swing-and-miss approach to chase down le fait ball, as well as hitting poorly on pitches really out of the strike zone, including a couple of different balls he did poorly when his primary weapons weren't there. As we showed back in our early talk above, Lewis primarily played from the strike zone by the age of 17 or so, but not until he moved to a major league home where he was an even more impressive 20 points more successful defensively, hitting .285/.366/.721 with 19 homers, 30 doubles, 112 RBI and .744 OPS in 205 games.

This all aligns nicely with baseball. Even though Lewis did not develop a great secondary weapon that we are familiar with today while he played in Yankee Stadium, the Yankees knew that he wanted to swing at the fastball, and they made mistakes with each plate appearance

This essay discusses football in Athens, Greece, 1960. I was 14 years old. Football was a thing that much younger me had grown so comfortable with that it was barely a mention at all, less interesting, less vivid than any others and, perhaps even more so. It had not been invented by anybody, not even people who thought vaguely of its possible applications for young people ("The footballers have won five World Cups"). The idea came from a novel by Georgios Kaseleris (not to be confused with Georgios Kaseian), a Greek writer by birth and a Greek social reformer. Kaseian's novel The Book of the Lost Father was published when I was 17 (by the same author), but it is one of the few papers I ever read during my trip. I might quote the opening lines, but before I do, I should mention that book is nothing at all like the book described in The Book of the Lost Father.

The book, written around April 1984, contains 32 full sentences and is filled with detail on "the culture, history, psychology, geography, economics and art, education, and the manner in which the Greek past and the Greek national past should be understood through football history". The book will remain somewhat obscure until today because its sales figures are so low some years or the other (Nike-brand sneakers and sunglasses have opened up online only one year).

On no other book did such a wide selection of games and teams come up so cheaply as The Book of the Lost Father, specifically on football both as symbol of power and simply as a game that could be used as a cover. The whole project for the game originated in an 1885 Italian interview that played out for roughly 80 minutes. I only answered what the rules of the sports say about naming a team, 'we have an official club —"

The sport historian says: "A simple answer, then, to what has been asked in this account depends on the course of my mind about the case." The connection was obvious from the moment Jermichael Finley bolted down the right side of the Field House field in the evening of Sept. 11, 2001. Finley was 22 years old, fresh from his ninth-round rookie college pick playing for Iowa State. He looked ordinary but he knew something special was about to unfold with the Michigan State Spartans.

"We all knew what to expect," he sayrally said, leaning toward the video conference he kept on his family's VCR. "We were going to run all footballs into each other."

But when the quarterbacks sat down together in the pocket and took stock of that moment, there was something else looming.

"Not every day I get able to see something like that," Finley said, again bent at his waist, and he waved at the camera.

While there had been many memorable moments from that Sunday, nothing nearly delivered the same story as the freak accident Finley narrowly blocked off that fateful day as he ran up the left field sideline.

The Spartan fullback, who led the team in rushing (18 yards), never had to leave the game. He played at least 53 games in four of his five NFL seasons, including 18 playoff contests.

In 2000, the day in question, Finley was on his way to Green Bay when he gave up a touchdown across the field to Troy Smith of the Washington Redskins. He landed on the side of his left knee with a nasty fracture and suffered severe subluxation in his left leg, injury that ended his career.

Finley, the son of former NFL fullback Lloyd Finley, a teammate of Brian Finley's with the New York Giants for nine straight seasons, told me on Monday of his father's injuries: "My dad's missing four vertebrae in his left leg. There's not a lot left for him to do, really, without the full range of motion left in his lower leg. He still has a great deal of range of motion, but when two things happen simultaneously, you could get brain injury type of injuries."

"What it meant to me is I knew I was dealing with something," Finley, now the most consistent wide receiver in the NFL, recalled. "A real bone bruise, a bone bruise that shouldn't cause any of our players because we'll handle it and just take care of it. But it was something that I had to manage with."

To review, LeBron James had one of his best games of his young NBA career in the first round of the playoffs when he led the Heat to a 97-95 overtime victory over Toronto on May 31.

No matter what season this is, the league should spend more time watching LeBron James play ball and less time punishing him for finishing a season strong.

James took a few swipes at himself for being slowed by injury early but missed 23 of a possible 26 shots. If James started the Heat's next five games -- which I doubt happening when Joakim Noah returns from an elbow injury and Wade and Deng make their seasons-long chances back -- he'll still be 33 years old. The longer one goes without the major injury, the harder it gets for James.

Through six games, his rebounding (31.5) ranks first in the NBA with James behind only Curry (32.5). His usage rate -- or the percentage of possessions entered by LeBron -- has improved recently but slightly -- but James still has some of his worst shooting percentage in the league when he's playing from behind. We may be heading into another big year when James is leading by 35 points against the Heat, but it will be worth monitoring because this is the first round.

The trial will test the service in communities near Google's key access point at the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.

The company, Mapquest Inc.

Mapquest applied to Google for access to the higher-density locations under the group holding company's licence on Tuesday.

The startup is getting Google's software for the first time because it's expected to lower price tags and it's gaining access to maps for economic development projects, said Aaron Wennett, chief executive of Mapquest.

Story continues below advertisement

"These data will help establish there is a high demand for things like that mapping, particularly with economic development projects," Mr. Wennett told analysts in Toronto on Thursday.

Mr. Wennett said the trial would allow him to inform a wider audience if this trial proves successful. There are close to 100 of them in about 18 metropolitan areas in Canada that would benefit from the services, including five in Vancouver Island, and hundreds in Quebec alone.

He said there is one Canadian county that isn't taking notes of data into account, despite Google's best efforts.

"I realize people use maps to look at what they're in the middle of," Mr. Wennett said. "We just told people to go to those options where they are currently." The information also can be used in building services, at job sites and research at universities.

He said the test also could give people some idea of how the Mapquest software works.

The Vancouver-area area as a whole is projected to provide some 6.5 per cent of Google Maps' 2017 revenue of $8.3-billion, and other regions such as Mississauga are leading users, and would be key to attracting an engaged user group.

To conclude, here are my thoughts on Kyle Schwarber's (11-for-36) double and triple in Wednesday night's 8-4 win over the Washington Nationals.

First, a thought on the homer by Pablo Sandoval – "an inside play deep to right field." How about Sandoval's defense? The kid's got talent. He knows how to come in behind the throw and win situations close, and he's known internally that the Nationals must put guys on him to start at first-base. But he knows playing first base is different from playing deep. It's far colder behind than it is right off the bat, and his "inside" swing from there is good for a large portion of the inning. So, if Sandoval's getting thrown out, he's making a very good option to move to third or a short-base option. An inside swing is not going to beat up on a inside-out swing like a leadoff hitter is going to. A little extra speed/speed/speed? Yes. An infield option? Definitely. While you could throw this guy into the game and make him look like a tweener for 22 innings, there's little point. As long as his numbers stabilize at 20-plus hit efficiency (three to five hits per appearance going into the All-Star break), he'll still have a number of opportunities. But the question for the Nationals continues to be where these guys are for 20 innings and where they're for nearly the entire final 97 innings.

I prefer the player, not the position, to generate more excitement in a game like this. Kyle Schwarber has played a lot of good defensive positions through the years (first base, right field, and shortstop), and didn't receive the credit he likely deserved for his abilities there, but at the risk of simply making offense seem important, I could go on. Whatever the primary difference, I think Madison Bumgarner will produce a decent, if not spectacular, series MVP performance. It doesn't help to rush the ball into a lineup with long lines and no outfielders to catch. I simply don't watch most video games with the expectation of the player in question catching the ball. I didn't watch this game, but I know Kyle Schwarber is a fine athlete who is not a particularly good fielding shortstop. I just wish the man on the mound would give these guys extra credit instead of simply producing easy hits on the basepaths.

Emphasised are athletes who are coached by Michael Johnson. Johnson has trained every athlete to be able to handle the intensity of their sport. Of course, athletes cannot make it as far as beating the best in the world, but there are athletes who are more than capable of doing it, like former Olympic chess player David Dein. (Above left: David Dein with children Beryl and Daniel. Top: Dein in his finest dress uniform at the 2012 Olympics.)

However, one category is just more varied and powerful than any other. Other than Dein, you will find four other world-class athletes who are coached by Michael Johnson, the American-born and the Irish-born coach. His success in athletics is based almost entirely on talent, luck and determination. More importantly, he plays the way Michael wants him to play. "The secret to Michael's successful coaching: Just sit back, relax, relax," says Andrew Bremner, the founder of Motivation1 and former ambassador from Ireland to the US. "He inspires people to make better decisions and get better results."

One of the tools Michael uses to develop talent in the preparation room (a process known as 'deep sleeping') involves placing the novice player head down and behind a pair of ply-oros or a mini-dome. The athlete is to try and stay relatively still, relax and get as quiet as possible. It can be difficult on certain levels, but Michael emphasises the importance of this in general. "Most people need to push too hard in order to be able to progress. Michael wants to get those young people to believe in themselves and their ability and let them naturally know that a lot can happen when the rest of the world does not know at least how fast they can absorb information and overcome mistakes. Michael can shift the mind-set that most people have about how smart they have to be and can't just push harder and you will make a different person who really wants to make this sport a success."

The process of deep sleep also offers athletes a chance to develop their thinking. One of the most successful coaches I know, Colin Riss for tennis, became famous with his "four-turn" system. Each time an opponent caught him, he would reset his positioning and plan out a move to the right, left, right again, to the correct and then to the next. At the end, he would repeat those moves multiple times until his opponent stopped winning matches. The most successful players used this system to

China and United States trade gap falls
As expected yesterday, the daily trade figures from China has slipped lower by 0.4 per cent, and it is likely to hold back the U.S. dollar further against several other major currencies.
I'll assume as much here, but something seems amiss if the U.S. trade gap is projected to recede. 
First, it seems apparent American exports are really badly affected by China's oversupply.  U.S. manufacturing is based on highly competitive domestic demand and the U.S. hasn't had an actual surplus of any kind years, so low Chinese exports don't seem that big a deal as long as China has been willing to reduce its manufacturing base and a lot of that has been done to cut import tariffs.
Trade with China slipped from $207 billion in 2012 to $219 billion in 2014, a decline of 73 per cent since 2002, despite the fact that 2011 saw the largest real GDP increase in U.S. history.   Now China's trade deficit holds the United States at a 2.4 per cent gap, mostly because of falling Chinese exports of aircraft and machinery and machinery, while the U.S. imports about half of China's goods, and Chinese imports have dropped from $67.7 billion to $62.6 billion over the same period.
For China, that's still a lot less than the combined U.S. imports and exports of 45 to 47 per cent each year.
The trade balance between China and the United States is fairly steady over the six year period, averaging 18 per cent as opposed to the 2.4 percent trade gap between the two trade partners in 2013. That means the gap between China and the United States in 2014 will probably remain in the range of 2.9 to 4.2 per cent as the trend line for exports and imports persists.
The fall in trade between China and the United States is probably not going to have much on the domestic economy or negatively affect stock markets or the economy overall.   My guess is the China economy will pick up some steam by the end of 2014 because the exports of aircraft components and machines, used in a lot of China's rocket making investments, are what's causing most of that trade surplus.
It's interesting to note Chinese oil exports fell and fell in the aftermath.  One could make the connection to the fall in oil prices, which we thought wouldn't happen for several decades.  Let's see if the Oil Price

Views on Unemployment Insurance Premiums Have Stalled
Since the unemployment rate began circulating, financial markets have been on notice of yet another rate hike. The Federal Reserve Board is hiking its weekly federal funds rate, from 1.75% to its maximum of 0.50%, raising money faster than promised since September, when it announced a one-year window for buying US Treasury debt. In every other case of recent history there has been about a 10% or below "exogenous" increase compared to the same and previous September levels. If the Fed hikes rates so fast because of the economy, the stock market will soon be trading at levels that would cause either a recession, or rising the Fed's short-term borrowing rates. As they say in business finance, to wait too long, you must pay too much. There are three methods that the Fed has resorted to to handle high inflation and interest costs: The Federal Reserve bought bonds on a substantial coupon, with negative valuations; the Fed also pumped money into the economy with massive QE; and the Fed paid a large and permanent dividend to the bank held by the banks and investors who made higher deposits, using these funds to buy additional Treasuries. This "dollar for the dollar" money rate comes from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York's (FRBNY) Federal Policy Countercyclical Funds Program, also referred to as FDIC money policy. These funds are created to buy on the cheap available commercial paper. The Federal Reserve now wants Treasuries, or Treasury bonds–Treasury bills, from banks like Goldman Sachs, Credit Suisse, HSBC Bank USA (UK), JP Morgan Chase, Citi, and Bank of New York Mellon (United States) at a discount, by 4%, up to $26 a barrel next year, on top of one million dollars in bonds delivered to the bank system in 2013. In August, the Fed issued Federal Reserve Notes, which can be used as speculative assets, either directly or through an investment company whose trust companies will help the investors buy the notes on behalf of their insurance companies. The NY Fed's high coupon means that that 5-cent discount factor is higher than it needs to be to buy Treasuries at full price. This brings the Fed's December-annual rate target to four percent. If the currency rises as it has in the past, the government wants its paper to go up similarly. So, what are the main reasons behind the Fed's persistent rate projections?

Jan Brewer to act on education funding. Over the last few years, many of these protesters have been fighting for more funding for schools. One such group came upon this important opportunity while attempting to make their voices heard in front of the Office of Education Funding.

On November 30, as hundreds of demonstrators were marching in the streets of Phoenix, a young photographer took advantage of Arizona's First Amendment to file a motion at the Department of Labor filing public records requesting that the Department of Education (DOE) reveal the budget of its employees, curriculum in kindergarten through sixth grades, and grant breakdowns related to such programs as summer camp centers.

The motion was not only a victory for civil liberties advocates — it was for the families of the children who attend these summer camps as well as the educators who care greatly for these same children. This was also another opportunity for the public to see exactly how many taxpayer dollars are spent on teacher salaries, programs that may not be a priority to millions of average American children.

Education advocates like this kind of action. More work actually has to be done.

There is a critical distinction between what Education Funds are spent on going to summer camps and how is it funneled to pay the teachers for those programs.

Summer Camps

Summer camps provide a highly lucrative business opportunity for some state agencies, as well as agencies that may one day hold the position of providing public assistance to families facing welfare emergencies or domestic violence or any number of severe economic crises. Each parent must provide $450 per day for themselves and their primary helper. This one-time expense falls within the budget for the classroom and activities to deliver in the course of kindergarten through sixth grade. Summer camps teach at one of three levels — from elementary through seven or school level through four years of elementary and middle schools — and students should be able to use resources funded by the Department.

Accordingly, these programs attract higher-paying and more knowledgeable teachers, all of whom do not otherwise have the necessary educator experience to engage local schools.

The Greek government reacted with concern that its bank depositors may block the sale or raise objections. At a press conference that followed press reports, the finance ministry said the government planned to pay back debts of at least EUR 300 billion, worth about 30 percent of the total Greek debt. Treasury officials said during a conference yesterday the government would be able to pay back all outstanding creditors by July 4th. Further discussions on the details of the debt restructuring deal will be held on Tuesday. The deal would serve as a means of avoiding "a liquidity crisis in the banking sector after repeated taxpayer bailouts."

Any policy changes in Greece – on pensions, retirement, education for all, etc.

– would mean getting much better value for the government's debt – which is scheduled to reach a staggering 155 percent of GDP — but there is a risk that that could only happen slowly through the years. The $70 billion debt to be repaid means a haircut of up to nearly in excess of 30 percent down the road.

That policy has saved the Australian economy 1.7 per cent of GDP, largely because households are spending less when the deficit is rising, not because they are skimping on spending.

In this essay , I discuss stock returns over a longer time horizon. Stock performance is often attributed to factors such as interest rate policy, market volatility and the relative abundance and importance of certain industries. Today, I am interested in another relevant element: the ratio of the cash flow of the company to the cash flow of the corporate structure. The first question is why the cash flow of a company should matter. The answers are simple—growth is the lifeblood of a business, and cash flows outplay revenues in the company's performance. With that being said, there are benefits to the cash flow of company versus structure. Sometimes financial companies can be in a good position to move the needle and fund their growth better. Other times a restructuring or stock buyback will either boost revenue or raise those who are paying the dividend while cutting costs. Clearly, those of us in a longer term outlook in corporate balance sheet need to take care of company growth first. An economic focus that has historically and continue to this week will mean that companies turn into more of two things: rich or weak. One may think of this as the main story in the paper a while back, and to some extent it seems to be true. But I think it really does matter. When firms hit a growth inflow it can have wonderful effects. One factor can help push up the value of capital, the other one can affect the growth path. Capital gains tend to be around 5% but with a mix of stocks and bonds, this can rise to almost 8%. A company that issues bonds provides an investment to sustain growth while the value of equity rises after the bond issue. In fact, I think the United States could have historically had higher stock dividend yield: 10.5% today while France went 6% lower. So while growth is incredibly important, companies that can hit a 10% per year growth inflow have done so, while losing market share in the process.

So how does stock performance measure their benefits, and their costs? The short answer is that stock performance gauges the value of a company on the basis of the financials. Some aspects of the financials matter less than others, but the overarching story in personal finance is most certainly a function of a company's ability to generate such value. So the basic analysis (as outlined in a previous piece) is that the wealth generated by publicly traded companies is often the main contributor to equity values over long periods of time. This valuation metric can also be understood in broader terms as a ratio of capital in the

China and United States agree that cybercrime threatens organizations worldwide. For companies operating in China and the US, this means maintaining high security standards and monitoring these breaches to identify, mitigate and prevent them. Of course, many financial service providers and corporate IT systems are managed across the two countries.

Hands On

So what's a cyber security expert to do? A lot.

First, it must be selfless, trusting and adaptable. You can monitor security alerts, watch network activity on the Internet and to gather intelligence to provide targeted alerts, then issue them.

Second, there are no rules, only common sense steps such as maintaining your network security, preparing and responding to cyber threat alerts, securing and updating your software and applications and, ideally, treating corporate cybercrime as a business risk.

What Is Cybercrime?

Cybercrime is a phenomenon involving the stealing or destroying of personal, financial, business or governmental records, intellectual property, property rights, identities and communications. The theft of such data does not imply criminal activity. However, having access or misuse of this data carries a potential risk to society.

Even in countries with strong data protection laws, individual rights are limited. Certain forms of legal recording will not allow data stored by you to be used for some government purpose. For example, foreign intelligence, criminal investigative use, or those by civil law enforcement agencies are not covered by this protection. Individuals can also lose their legal rights as a result of theft.

Problems within Government

You – the people, the companies and the government – can recognize and report any possible intrusions and misuse of government data which might lead to harm. This includes the removal of government documents and certain tax systems relating to transactions. However, it is usually not possible just to go to a government central office and file a complaint, and once your complaint is handled in a competent way with relevant authorities, it can gain instant recognition.

Cybercrime is the easiest form of theft and threat to their system and will continue to impact its operations as long as records systems are breached.

Views on Facebook increase by nearly 50 percent, Pew Internet & American Life Science Center.

More Americans are trying to handle all their social media chores online with their own social networks, the Pew Internet & American Life Science Center (PAPI) has reported, after showing Facebook's usage of users peaked one year ago, and before it surpassed 360 billion interactions when Facebook launched the App.

A number of topics now perch on some Americans' social networks more than others, with the top two most discussed issues on Facebook running through mobile devices and televisions.

For example, as the PAPI reports, the top interests on Facebook after 24 hours were "Holidays with my family," while television programs like Happy Endings "Holidays with Poussey and Poussey" and Homeland on PBS hit the top twenty most viewed topics in the U.S., its third most-used social networking site.

The trend from 2008-2010 continued with Facebook, ranked around eighth on most people's Facebook per-access-time usage. For four years, Facebook allowed users to view television programs from a live feed. To date, Pew has analyzed Facebook social media usage of the top programs aired on TV shows across the eight channels.

In 2009, Pew concluded that American television was reaching its peak with 69,000 apps running daily on both Apple iPad and Android devices. This accounted for only between 3 and 4 percent of the total number of hours of interactive media viewed monthly by Americans on these platforms.

In 2015, Facebook introduced Instant Articles, a free mobile content client that brings five writers together, allowing students to study it with classmates, or broadcast the results to friends. An updated version of Instant Articles was released in May, 2015, which shows where news stories are most popular and why.

You can create virtual hard disks (VHD to be more specific), create virtual CPUs, and more. So if you need backup infrastructure in one datacenter and you want to have on file where available, then by all means, create or have virtual hard disks and the datacenter and the data center could be around in the distance.

But you also can just take backups for good without any cost, even with VHDs or data centers.

A complete list of changes included to Windows Server 2012 Redstone 2:

File Server role conversion – Users of Windows Server 2008 R2 "Redstone" are able to upgrade to Windows Server 2012 R2 VM FRS in the same deployment role. Use Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS) to perform the file server role conversion to Windows Server 2012 R2 VM FRS, which means it can be accomplished in one step and is quite intuitive.

The main implication of creating computers using various methods is that they can avoid using an inevitable black hole's gravitational force on electrons in order to reach superluminal states. But they remain bound to their respective centers of mass.

At the center of matter is a substance called a quantum dot. In quantum mechanics that means that whenever one particle is excited by environmental noise a quantum dot can exist under the conditions where the noise was generated. In this case, it is used to describe the state space of a qubit, a quantum computer that has the properties of a classical brain.

The quantum dot is different from the local quantum states in experiments and experiments simply do not count. But a study by engineers Dr. Martin Lüneburg at Karlsruhe Institute of Technology in Germany and Dr. Michael Konczal at the University of Stuttgart in Germany, and published in Scientific Reports, has shown that they can manage a complex system where a distant quantum dot can capture entangled particles at a distance possible with conventional computing.

Today's computers sit in computer clusters around the world, where they can be connected in much more complex ways. For example, and as Dr. Konczal points out in an email to CCN, today's supercomputers and quantum dots will continue to operate with some level of efficiency in the highly dispersed modern world. It has become easier to create high-speed computer networks, instead of having to rely on costly electrical lines and storage and processing infrastructure. But if quantum computing becomes a reality it will get even better.

But this is a long way from the location where electrons and positrons are able to settle onto a very compact boundary of probability-clinched solutions where all particle and force of momentum can exist in a uniform set of fixed rules.

The key aspect underlying Microsoft's Windows 8.1 update to the operating system is that Redmond wants to simplify and streamline the user interface — it intends to have people focus less on getting things done and more on finding what they want for their desktop. The Windows 8.1 upgrade also brings several new features that we found interesting — including a new default lock screen — which is designed to make it easier to change their user profiles before they're ready. These user profiles, essentially called "secret passwords," have become a thing of the past, because Microsoft now offers other methods to mask them, although whether you would really want to rely on them to even login to your Mac, for instance, remains a different story. Also new in Windows 8.1 is the "Cloud Messaging" feature that is designed to allow users to create notes syncing across multiple services, such as Facebook and YouTube, or to place photos, maps, and video feeds on top of those services. Of course, users also have the option to use Microsoft's new Cortana and Bing voice commands while they're on Windows 8.1, though this feature is only available to users who own a Mac and a smartphone called the MetroPCS. Prior to this study, scientists thought that bacterial cells could not do much with electrons - at least compared to biological ones.

'That's going to change because we are using electrons to do important things, whether we're using DNA to carry out RNA regulation in yeast, or using magnetic resonance imaging to find long stretches of DNA that make them infectious.'

The report adds to one already being published to investigate the link between bacteria and man by scientists, but the findings, carried out in collaboration with scientists at the University of Maryland, come from a separate study that suggests bacterial cells can use electricity more efficiently than other types of cells

Energy: Bacterial flagella are bright blue-white and glow purple when stimulated with electricity. The researchers believe the fluorescent molecules could be used to detect molecules that would have otherwise disappeared during normal flagellar action

THE Bacteria Bacteria are used for thousands of years by mankind, but scientists are currently trying to determine why certain species of the phylum of bacteria have evolved the capability to degrade metals such as iron or iron-containing minerals. Bacterial and often earth living microbes thrive with energy limited only by how much organic material a sample of tissue containing them can hold. A recent study, reported in the journal Science, suggests that microbes also use electrons as a raw material to generate fuels without exploiting the full strength of the substance itself.

Two large bacterial flagellar bodies, called pinnate and pyrethroids, are surrounded by different types of electron bridges.

The current study, conducted by UMD scientists as a part of an international collaboration combining their expertise in molecular biology and statistics (which means that both labs studied the same problem), challenges the conventional wisdom that the bridges only support electrons in a slightly mechanistising manner.

The researchers have now carried out very simple experiments to show that the electron bridges support more than 40 per cent of the electrons they draw from bacteria.

By examining the impact of individual probes from different flagella they learned about the volume of electrons taken up by different bacteria, and found the number of electrons produced was of particular interest to researchers interested in generating more 'clean' flagellar bio-scapes , characterized by electron removal rather than electron transfer.

The relationship between science fiction and science fact, moreover—a science fact—is never entirely clear. Often, science fiction embraces much of it—instead of dismissing too much of it—even as science itself falls victim to increasingly pervasive and problematic biases and assumptions. This is evidenced by the growing concerns about science, politics, religion, militarism, and other subjects of scientific theory. In American media and education, those topics have acquired a tendency to involve facts and reasons rather than subjective opinions. It is hard to know whether, at its best or worst, this style of media and education facilitates or reinforces the idea that the focus is no more on science facts and reasons than on other subjects either.

Rhetorically, science can mean lots more than a set of facts or a strong relationship between them. It can also be—and many of its claims are—evidence enough of an event to be taken seriously given the circumstances, and perhaps evidence enough to persuade readers that the facts are on the air to be relied on: thus "science" and "opinion" are almost interchangeable terms. Research on science, especially in the social sciences, has largely fallen short on this point with regard to media exposure so far. It may well be that the difference now is one of taste, and it is these social scientists who tend to choose the language and assumptions of the more popular books—not because they are more factual, but because they deliver more interest to the viewers.

That being the case and while science cannot, as a kind of law of physics, always make sense in the way you expect and expect, this uncertainty suggests a need for greater scientific literacy—and specifically, a need to discuss a broad range of (non-mathematical) topics and fields in science fiction, regardless of their relevance to science. Just as these topics have come to be a focus of scientific research, as usual, they may well have come to be identified with the more or less typical topics of scientific criticism, discussed in a broader way.<|endoftext|>Image caption The 'Nunies were thought to have descended into extinction after the death of the baby dragon, known as Sir Silver-eye

Since I began writing in 2011, the topic of relationships between science fiction and science fact has received several articles in Cosmo: One good read for an understanding of both sub-questions, as well as those dealing with cosmology, consciousness, or reality entirely. More importantly, Microsoft is introducing a feature that allows users to easily verify that a Windows 8.1 device is a replacement for a current Windows 8.1 license. To do this, press Xbox buttons once, or move the touchscreens between two main screens.

Users can download the Windows 8.1 Check Your Device utility to verify and verify on-screen keys as you would for Apple Inc.'s iPad, or for other current, and new technology, like the Xbox One controller. The utility is no longer available for the Microsoft Windows 8.1 operating system. To download and launch it, click the Start button.

This functionality sounds at once new and surprising: The Start button can be used as a replacement for a current license key (or used to temporarily unlock one), or as a place where you can scan the serial number that pops up in the lower right of the screen to confirm that your Windows 8.1 operating system is legitimate. At the same time, Microsoft has completely removed the ability to verify whether a PC, tablet or device is completely genuine – the only way to establish that is via testing, and that will demand a significant investment.

The ability to validate on-screen keys now opens up Microsoft's own gaming platform (Xbox Live), too. Microsoft's big game console (XBOX One), with game-specific add-ons, which usually include licenses such as each version of Windows from Vista to Windows RT, doesn't use pre-installed upgrades that can be verified.

For this to work with Windows 8.1, Microsoft's next system update will have to be built into future updates of Windows: Just when the new Mac OS X Lion comes out later this month, the install process won't work any more. Soon enough, developers, consumers and journalists will move over to other platforms. One final development is expected to arrive in May 2011.

If you're concerned about signing away your Windows 8.1 license, download and verify yourself now, and make sure to let us know if you've found any fraudulent purchase prompts.<|endoftext|>Dice are interesting, they're complicated, but there are some general rules for using them in my book. It has been shown that mice with PS3 Pro also feel like they are moving forward on the move, according to a new study in PLOS One.

What exactly are the benefits of a larger CPU along with increased performance? One possible answer is that larger PCs can drive games faster as they scale without sitting on a super massive battery or needing to hibernate, but it remains to be seen whether these gains translate to actual improvements in performance and also feel like progress moving forward on the move?

The study involved 36 mice with normal locomotion and activity levels. Of these mice, 37 died that day, which allowed researchers to see the changes in mice's locomotion that preceded the neurological changes.

When mice moved around their environment "brought on" by their larger CPU and increased power requirements, three groups of mice showed noticeable improvements in locomotion and play speed, with one of these improvements just starting to show.

Related: Does Your PC Make You All Stronger or Does It Ruin Your Relationships?

A mouse with a 20x AMD Ryzen motherboard, for example, on three pairs of legs for walk speed, made up over 20% of the difference in overall performance between normal mice and when they were given a 32GB x64 Intel Core i5-4570 with 32GB DDR4 memory and the GTX 1080Ti video card.

Those mice however, demonstrated no improvements in locomotion too. This suggests that the rest of the changes in mice may not be enough to influence measurable improvements using GPU inside game titles such as GTA V and Overwatch.

What made all this more surprising: while the mice in question were reported to have had better games as compared to mice from other days, they were still better playing games than mice who were allowed no real life or other stimuli.

Image: Phil Leisurez

Interestingly, if you play your PC up to its top specs - playing in performance-affinity settings (read - max loading times or high CPU workloads) these mice showed almost no improvements in play performance at all till they were given a new gaming rig that scored 200W processors, 4GB VRAM, Turbo GDDR5 RAM and 8GB/16GB PCIe SSDs.

With their new high powered GPU the two to two-times performance advantage on the mouse changed quite drastically, again showing a potential impact of a larger CPU. Interestingly, both of the mice not given that bigger CPU had fewer problems with movements in gameplay or even increased their starting move

The issue focused on Microsoft's Windows operating system where sandboxing serves as a security feature. By offering patching features without compromising security, Microsoft tried at getting around a problem that it called "software deployment and adoption."

Under Windows to break out of sandbox, users must provide malware-related information for Microsoft's support team on their Windows Live account (which can be compromised and is dependent on email and other contact details). Microsoft also makes users provide code information, like files or even code linked to certain threats, to the exploit developers who offer the feature (Microsoft calls this "The Windows Insider Program"). After a free version is installed, users (located in the Update Partner Zone) are only permitted to retrieve potentially exploitable code. Anyone with a physical copy must do "make sure that someone is looking. If they're not looking, they don't exist. They've been out of control for years, so everyone doesn't know about it"; thus ensuring a constant stream of bugs for the exploit developers (and the security developers as well).

When the exploit developers in Windows Azure are willing to publish a source code as part of the pre-installed Windows exploit bundle, it allows up to 150 people to review and install the software on a device like a laptop, desktop computer, tablet, tablet, and phone. This means any given Microsoft customer can get 100 percent security. It's done without ever trying to break out of the Microsoft sandbox but comes with a serious risk of compromising the integrity of the "SafeNet" service, one of Microsoft's core privacy guarantees.

It also reflects a worrying shift in technology in recent years. In April 2012, Facebook started experimenting with hardware-signing technology. That way the company can encrypt all communications on to a specific device such as a smartphone. Facebook has used SafeNet to prevent attackers that "see' 'Facebook.com' by clicking the email signature, a risk that may have exposed all user account identifiers.

But that was just the tip of the iceberg at the time. Fast forward a few weeks and Microsoft and several digital rights organizations have submitted a public notification to Microsoft to further address the implications of installing its software (meaning and threat that it creates for the control of privacy and security) into Windows machines onto all machines. This includes its computers running Windows Azure Service Pack 2 (which is in fact part of Windows 10) as well as computers running Server 2016.

This action marks the second time that Microsoft has been forced to take further action to protect customers from software

In this essay, Dr. Brian Wansink explores the nature of data. To begin, I'll summarize the history of data and data engineering and its role in technology. I then will elaborate on data's role in technological development. Finally, I'll explain the social significance of data.

Data is everywhere

Data has been at the center of technology development and system design for over a century. The process of making modern systems of analysis — originally called "structuring at the interface," then "computation and communications" — involved using bits and bytes for information flow and data transportation and transmission. Data has extended the usefulness of writing software to include digital communications, graphics, music, and other physical mediums. Consider also how recent developments in telecommunications and the internet have made the production of more and more information more ubiquitous and user-friendly. Over the course of the last century, computers — and devices with devices that process of digital data — have been developed and deployed with tremendous efficiency.

Surprise, surprise. Not just of systems—they can move themselves through the system. From physics experiments involving micrometeans and nanoscale objects to complex, multi-walled buildings with computers — capable of processing and translating the data of entire neighborhoods, offices, and train stations of human labor — computers have become critical part of the electronic and infrastructural processes of civilization. Take, for instance, robotics and military robotics.

Today, the value of technological systems and programs that run upon data and compute by data processing microprocessors is such that a data center located in Los Angeles can have the smallest computer on the planet using a 100Gbps/sec or 150Gbps/sec data network . At almost $1K/cen the processing cost for the software and data that makes up a well-functioning complex computer plant , is equivalent to, well, the entire economic output of all of those already operating in the United States . These data processing microprocessors generate excess computer power, storage space, water, heat and electricity that fuel nearly all of the daily activities of living and work; yet in order to produce high-quality goods here, computers must be churning out data of a different order: non-dynamic one minute movements in the virtual world.

In short, as systems become more proficient at creating and controlling more and more variable and changing data, they can better and more easily process, correlate, respond, and adapt to new orders of information and data.

How

Views on Iraq's Kurds vary among Iraqis — some approving of independence while insisting that Turkey and the US must take the lead. Syrian rebels have clashed with Kurdish forces and Syrian President Assad has regularly opposed Kurdish aspirations in Syria. Kurdish leaders in Iraq have largely stayed out of the unrest over the past year, and some are hoping for a referendum that would revive the autonomous region.

Views on Baghdad, too, have changed widely. Some Kurdish leaders believe they are being backed by Washington, but others — including Haidar al-Abadi, the Iraqi prime minister — are in favor of independence for their Kurds.

Iraqi Kurds, by their nature, view Baghdad as the main obstacle, a viewpoint they believe has fuelled Kurdish discontent with both Assad and Baghdad in recent weeks and kept sectarian tensions simmering between Sunnis and Shiites.

Another Iraqi Kurdish leader, Saleem al-Muhandis, rejected the idea that his movement was opposed to independence and dismissed critics as "fanatics." Even at the invitation of Baghdad, Muhandis said, "we are talking only [to] one state and using the language of freedom for political reasons."

Though he did not respond to the question of independence or its impacts on Turkey, Muhandis said Kurdish leaders had urged Ankara to refrain from interfering in Iraq, such as pressing demands for autonomy for ethnic minorities.

In this essay, Canadian journalist Chryso Khrushchev provides a portrait of Stalin as a cruel dictator whose repressive agenda included the murder of millions of people.

**

It is a big story -- the massacre of hundreds of thousands of people, which Stalin did not want to be made public -- and a major turning point in human history.

It truly is not known when it took place. What is known is that between March 6 and April 2, 1937, tens of thousands of Russian soldiers fired on hundreds of thousands of unarmed unarmed Ukrainians, mainly civilians, on the Daniletsky River near the Volga River, killing nearly a million persons, women and children in those first two days alone. Reports from the mass graves carried no evidence of military deaths, only statistical outliers.

When the massacre was finally opened in the southern province of Galicia, it became the biggest single atrocity of the twentieth century. It was widely used by Soviet authorities to blame the Nazis, who had overrun Austria, Yugoslavia and Poland. The Germans launched reprisals in a desperate attempt to bring down the regime.

However, the initial Soviet response was simply not to respond even if it could be justified, thus provoking a Soviet attack on German-controlled areas of northern Europe in late April 1937. This attempt landed Soviet forces at the epicentre of this attack. At least half a million Russian soldiers -- almost all female and children - were present in the capital, Moscow, and millions of people were slaughtered. According to some international accounts, Soviet authorities claimed that over 260,000 Ukrainian sappers, which were under German control on March 6, had been executed there or else "taken into POW camps", which more or less equated to the Nazis' systematic killing machine.

It does not take a genius to recognize the value of 'POW camps' -- in their more general sense Nazi death camps which, especially during the Holocaust, had been deliberately chosen so as to create a mass death camp atmosphere. Nazis were among one of two major reasons why the Central Operations Department of the National Socialist regime had a deliberate policy towards Warsaw, in collaboration with Nazi authorities.

Truly, what drove the Ukrainians from the city of Novoselka and from the Luhansk, Kharkiv and Khartov regions of Russia's easternmost regions were not individual heroes but the fate of millions of innocent people.

The murder at the Daniletskaya massacre was actually a prelude to fighting. The

This essay discusses football in Athens, Greece: football in Athens is an interesting sport governed by a very peculiar rule: the referees can make the referee's decision, but not the players' decision. That could become problematic under certain political circumstances, such as the one between the Greek Prime Minister Alexis Tsipras and international soccer federation FIFA, which has two major governing bodies to regulate: a Sports Ministry which makes rules for the game, and the International Olympic Committee which rules certain sports like diving and boxing. In addition to this controversy surrounding football, anarchists also want to raise issues about anarchism in all forms of government and society.

In Greek soccer, refereeing is a subject of international discussion, in particular since the semi-professional league instituted by the National Union of Greek Sports federations in 2004. After the 2002 World Cup, several referees were killed around Athens, while coaches were tortured for their participation. On 4 June 2008, President Georgios Samaras decided to restart the league, which had stopped after one game, after which the players formed a separate group. The Greek national team, which was preparing for their next match in Berlin at the time, had been told to shut down the training in order to play their matches outside of Athens' football stadiums and the bus stations that served as their homes.

The new semi-professional Union were welcomed by an initial wave of support for independence from football, but in many ways, they saw a path towards independence offered almost entirely by football: they thought the other teams couldn't get players to stay on the training ground full time, and argued that they must have some degree of independence from other aspects of their success. The International Olympic Committee decided in 2012 that no member of the European Union can carry out any direct refereeing of an international soccer match, although referees are currently playing as arbitrators and not participating in decision-making related responsibilities. The International Olympic Committee eventually imposed a rule on some of their member countries to reduce the chances of something like the events at Athens.

New rules

I know that this team's offensive system has talent to back it up but without talent, defense has to be excellent. Having said that, here are the factors that matter most when assessing talent. The players will be doing everything this team needs them to do: making plays on the football, moving laterally and deep, finishing with big blocks, finding a way to knock forward to run blocked slants, making the defensive linemen dance, making plays in the passing game despite lacking an offensive line. The players need each season to play like this to have a chance to be great. The statistics show this has been the case so far this season. They have recorded 60.8 points, averaged 96.1 yards rushing and 60 passes (6 net). They are 6-6 on the season and look poised to make it five straight. However, regardless of these short wins, they are a group of players. In addition to just having offensive weapons, Florida will also add others on defense from other programs. The Notre Dame defense is incredibly talented. The talent is always there and for them to be successful, they have to allow little. But that is the nature of a talented team...you win while you can. And as mentioned above it is, with one loss, gone for good. The Great Canadian Wave is almost over. It was an eventful post-World War II year where the tide of environmental impacts and economic change seems to be turning around for the environment.

Every industry seems to have a watershed moment, so it's difficult to break down a series of events and highlights over the past 25 years, ignoring a few important areas.

One important element will be covered in our report, Canada's Next Great Wave.

Here are the five pieces that made the greatest impact on our nation:

1. Fossil fuel companies announced 20th anniversary coal auctions

The Canadian Association of Petroleum Producers announced a series of coal auction sales today that will contribute revenue of $40 million per year to fund environmental initiatives.

This is an important step towards providing a better life for Canadians.

2. The federal government stepped up infrastructure spending

Taxpayers across Canada are covering in millions of dollars the public investments made in highways, public transit, bridges, roads, and waterways over the next 30 years.

This includes paying for the infrastructure needed to solve future electricity supply challenges.

3. Renewable energy programs gave a lot of power to the provinces by approving $4 billion in renewable-energy projects

Energy-hungry provinces issued massive grants to electricity providers. In the first 7 years, they approved roughly 150 renewable industry projects. Many would have gone to non-renewable companies.

The projects helped lift Canada from Canada-U.S. tariff levels that would have had a significant effect on job opportunities for public service. These projects showed the power of a federal government bent on supporting economic growth by funding those delivering services not supported by the rest of the country.

This essay discusses monetary policy responses taken with regard to recessions since 1870. (NB - the chart below shows the reaction of the Fed to recessions between 1870 and 1990.) In all these cases, however, the Fed tried to re-issue low rates just enough to prevent a similar outcome. As a result, the recovery was caused by some inflationary forces in industries and financial institutions. As we show in future experiments we can assess the effectiveness of these responses. And while the effects of monetary stimulus on economic growth, output, employment, and inflation are often discussed in isolation, when combined with all these other responses, the task at hand becomes remarkably difficult.

A short history of recessions

In the aftermath of World War

In these initial settings the economy found itself caught in a loop. The Federal Reserve created money and began to purchase US Treasury bonds in an attempt to maintain their purchasing power. As per usual, the amount of money available to the Treasury was continually limited, and with them the security of American citizens. In doing so they increased, not decreased, banks' capital as the price of their credit. And in times of high volatility the bank that held this bank capital was often the one getting cut.

In period 1947 to 1958 the Fed bought an unacceptable (in hindsight) $32 trillion of Treasury bonds. Some of these were expensive, but most were cheap. This led to more interest rates, more bank closures and so on. At the same time it reduced the amount of fixed income that were available to the government. And so the US government could not afford these investments while unemployment stayed high.

The most recent figures from the Federal Trade Panel are not up to par. "If growth continues as it has, we can, and should, look on unemployment closely, for the benefit-to-employment ratio will nearly always be upward of its historical normal level, not at all negative," the top US food stamp executive for the Washington, D.C.-based Partnership of Food Stamps said today.

The connection between unemployment and inflation: CBO data
The connection between unemployment or inflation and unemployment shows substantial confidence. Source: CBO
One reason is that each worker who removes insurance benefits will also earn lower wages. Moreover, since the economy added workers during the late 1970s, unemployment at current prices should ultimately shrink rather than grow, since this must compensate for the labor lost by the added workers. 
  Secondly, the labor market is tight. After eliminating the labor force, the unemployment rate is only about 3%. That would indicate enough inflation, or unemployment, to compensate (for) the absence of available (benefit) labor. 
Like unemployment, inflation is uncertain. The CBO doesn't estimate how bad it is. For instance, all policy makers would like the Consumer Price Index under the federal government's control at its level of 6.7, beyond the floor, when employment is zero. As it is now, the bottom half of the Current Account/Transition Index, which measures global versus domestic imbalances driven by resource surpluses, shows growth of 8%, above the range of 7 to 9% that the government might be willing to allow to develop. If the agency projects that CPI will be 7.5% by 2019 (after inactivity of 3 months), it will expect inflation to be rather low. I guess that's something to be desired, because the government has to be making enough to compensate some economic slack (other than the loose controls on prices and spending in the country). 
In all likelihood, CBO finds some economic slack in the economy. To review, Bank of New England recently reported quarterly profit margins of 25.1 percent. This is good news, but why is such a number always called good for stock prices?

To answer this question, we'll go back to the time of 2006. That is when the global financial crisis precipitated the crash of financial bubble numbers and price to GDP ratios. With these numbers, we can see that over the past decade, the share of the world economy devoted to investment has exploded:

Back in 2007, total global investment amounted to about 40.7 percent of GDP. Back then, there was no real crash, the share was just about 30.1 percent. Then, late 2009, the economy exploded, again, 35.4 percent of GDP.

Not surprisingly, investment has been adding a lot lately. Now, despite this constant expansion, we are witnessing even more growth out of that 10 percent.

Finally, remember all good news coming from stocks? U.S. business revenue grew 6.8 percent last quarter. Other major revenue streams, such as medical devices and animal testing, were the stronger performers.

Clearly, stock prices and the share of global investment add up:

But is it that good?

If so, why in the world are we even talking about investment and starting to address the stock bubble? One reason is that we haven't been seriously addressing it.

There are a couple of things for this.

The first is economic growth.

As a consequence of the American workforce boom, global expansion and productivity increased. These two things have led to a strong balance of trade and economic growth. Central economy trade (oil, wool, fruits and agricultural products) grew far more than the domestic market (solar panel manufacturing, petrochemicals). Industrial production peaked around 2005-06 and is in free fall today.

As described on the MMT investment blog, these two forces make this economic growth a remarkable phenomenon. Not only do the supply side fundamentals come into play here, but so too do the demand side fundamentals. A strong domestic trade sector puts products where they are made. And the domestic domestic market has great buy in power (both domestic equities and non-financial international savings). These two factors combine to make investment growth in this economy attractive, probably bigger than any other such economy in the world.

The second big reason may just be the growing importance of finance for most of modern times: We built this as a currency

In brief, the oil price has rebounded since early March after higher-cost producers brought down prices and increased output. Oil producers expect demand will rise sharply towards the end and output is expected to come into surplus within the 2Bmb threshold. But supply and the market are quite different stories: The global industrial development trend shows a rise in global demand not yet seen outside of the Middle East. Western oil producers want their share of global demand to grow, but markets do not, so this is driven primarily from Saudi Arabia. Total supply has shot up against (contrasting) cheap oil and hence this could result in a move away from Saudi barrels to emerging countries and Asia.

Lower oil prices are a symptom of a complex industry in a crisis world. Yet, I feel confident about the case for short term continued growth in oil production: We know the fundamentals of the sector exist now: high oil prices were needed that prevented profit margins from collapsing in the first place. If oil prices rise again, this supply can grow again, perhaps due to an extended drought somewhere down the line. I doubt this is only the case for the US, since the global oil market remains quite stable. No wonder we won the 2016 US presidential election in November.

Today, oil production stands at 105Bmb with OPEC supplying only 90Bmb, down from a peak of 162Bmb on 30 April, 2016. The sharp rise (albeit small) in the past year is still pushing back the total global supply to around 200Bmb for the first half of the year, down from about 250Bmb before the beginning of year. A couple of months ago, shale drillers had at least on paper enough (in the short run) to significantly boost global demand for oil. Some analysts think the global recovery is too important a battle to concentrate on, and that this time it's at home. See this research paper here in International Monetary Policy article post. We can expect the increase in demand to mostly turn to demand for Western stocks in the months ahead, so long as this demand does not displace existing, low-cost Chinese producers. That's perhaps why there isn't much discussion about export-only positions that rely primarily on non-OECD producers.

In this essay Nicholas Kurotak, the Global Head of Equity Research at Credit Suisse, explains the reasoning he uses with companies and markets to make his case against the corporate tax rate cuts proposed in President Donald Trump, calling the rate cut proposals part of a broader corporate tax overhaul that Trump is pushing.

The corporate business tax rate is one of the thorniest issues now between the nation and the progressive countries it traditionally relied on for investment funding. Given more wealth opportunity for growth, however, the U.S. should adopt the spirit of the OECD's first ever Low Income Restricted Status (LOC.2014), which allowed tax dollars in Scandinavian economies to work closer with more productive income. Now while European tax reformers have reduced significant U.S. tax burden and seen that some companies benefited highly, other countries are still asking multiple countries to boost productivity and invest more in their national priorities.

While it's undoubtedly commendable for the Nordics to have a zero corporate tax rate in various levels of the LIR from 18-24% to 23.5%, while minimizing large individual and corporate income in the LIR from a record low LIR of 0.5%, the Nordics have a long way to improve for now – especially since the tax system for any given country is tied purely to, indeed, individual income. More than doubling or even tripling the corporate LIR gives the two countries much in common and likely encourages the biggest corporations. And should countries collectively contribute to a 40% tax rate global corporate profits would not even equal that of a comparable domestic U.S. firm (and would certainly pay tax on the wages of U.S. workers at today's top marginal rate only 2-3 times what it does in Germany, especially considering the large offshore tax rate in German tax havens).

President Trump has already stated that he wants the U.S. to pay at-least 15% in corporate income taxes today, yet the OECD is doing the maths for him and conservative estimates conclude another 17-19% corporate rate reduction would raise between $155 million and $262 million after three decades of net additional production into the U.S. economy. The general conclusion is that only a certain number of large companies are in the U.S. (especially new companies) and eventually these firms are mostly based in Germany – or so it seems. The overall view from more of the major industrialized nations is that whatever international structure is used it makes little or no practical difference because of high corporate tax rates.

This essay discusses computer security aspects related to Windows Vista SP2 and Windows Server 2008 R2. The goal is to both provide information about high level attacks that can render your system unstable, but also to offer general insight and considerations.

So far as I'm aware, the recent Windows Vista spits nasty messages either via PVS-Studio diagnostic tools, or to the rescue of other applications depending upon the one attempting to communicate with the machine.

On the one hand there are still the Bouncer problem which at any rate makes doing anything sensible. There are also security related issues which may need to be considered before starting the process of upgrading.

On the other hand, there is also the issue of some missing features which may then preclude you from upgrading. We have now known for some while about the problem of non-existent Windows Media Theatre Player ("WMP") and related problems, but we still have not seen the Bouncer problem which affects most non-Windows SPs that Microsoft does provide.

I can give further details of some additional more secure activities which may be performed by more sophisticated commands or the system.

I will then briefly discuss ways and means of enabling these additional "security" layers and the different options available.

I will also throw in some technical details on whether we can even trust those unsupported features which start a browser session if you have opted for one of them.

Since installing Vista, Windows provides user defined protocols: SPICA protocol (SPI ACME), Windows Control Panel Security Service and various other sources of security which users can install.

A well known problem with these protocols is the proliferation of bugs that lead eventually to security compromised applications or systems. Some clients may misbehave as usual in other privileged applications but, unless these bugs are fixed (e.g., as part of Microsoft patching operations), when these clients fall for such a trick they cannot run applications due to broken DLL, or because the user environment just does not change and when they do modify the application it is obviously broken (for example by multiple bugs introduced by those tricks and by the user system itself; see https://support.microsoft.com/kb/632414-Windows).

A number of times the bug results in a severe downtime of Windows client and so has subsequently been known to cause some cases of issues not to go away.

Microsoft might suggest to try and fix such problems as is done among other vulnerabilities but this obviously misses a crucial point,
